# Case Study Template

> Sherpa case studies use a distinctive four-section format. The "What Was Hard" section is a differentiator. Every competitor publishes sanitized success stories. Sherpa includes friction because honesty is a design principle.

```yaml
---
client-type: startup | scale-up | enterprise | non-profit
engagement-type: ai-literacy | agentic-workflows | governance-setup | full-adoption
tools-used: []
timeline: "[duration, e.g. 6 weeks]"
anonymous: true
---
```

---

## The Situation

[What the organization was dealing with. 1-2 sentences. Be specific — "a 200-person fintech with 3 failed AI pilots" not "a leading organization facing AI challenges." If anonymous, use descriptive references: industry, size, and the problem.]

## What We Did

[Specific actions, tools used, timeline. Name the framework components, the workshop format, the deliverables. Concrete enough that a reader could estimate scope.]

## What Happened

[Measurable results where possible. Percentages, timelines, adoption numbers. If hard metrics aren't available, describe observable changes — "the team shipped their first governed workflow within two weeks" is better than "improved AI maturity."]

## What Was Hard

[Honest about friction, trade-offs, and surprises. What didn't go as planned? What took longer than expected? What would you do differently? This section is what makes Sherpa case studies credible.]

---

## Blind Spots (Optional)

[What gap did the engagement close that the client didn't know existed? What new opportunities emerged that weren't in the original scope? This section turns a case study into a learning artifact.]

---

*See also: [Content Guidelines — Case Studies](../ux/content-guidelines.md#case-studies), [Voice & Tone](../ux/voice-and-tone.md), [Content Brief Template](./content-brief.md)*
